data.title: The Ithaka Group Q4 2025 Performance, Contributors And Detractors
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from The Ithaka Group Q4 2025 Commentary

In a slightly positive market, Ithaka's portfolio lagged the R1000G during the fourth quarter, trailing the index by 700bps (-5.9% to +1.1%, gross of fees). Stock selection detracted 740 bps to relative performance, which was slightly offset by a 40bp positive impact from sector allocation. The portfolio demonstrated weak breadth and depth with only 8 of the 29 stocks held for the entire quarter, representing 28% of the names and 26% of the total weighting, outperforming the benchmark.

4Q25 Performance

Performance (%) 4Q25 1 YR 3 YR 5 YR ITD 1
Ithaka US Growth Strategy (Gross) (5.9) 5.1 29.1 8.9 17.2
Ithaka US Growth Strategy (NET) (6.1) 4.5 28.3 8.3 16.7
Russell 1000 Growth ("R1000G") 1.1 18.6 31.2 15.3 17.7
S&P 500 TR Index 2.7 17.9 23.0 14.4 14.8
1 ITD = inception-to-date, annualized. Inception date is 1/1/2009.

At the sector level, Ithaka generated positive relative returns in only one of the six major growth sectors where we maintain active exposure: Materials and Processing. Outperformance in materials was due to a sole holding purchased in the quarter outperforming the index. Our largest area of underperformance was Technology where the underperformance was pervasive, with only 4 of the 15 positions held for the entire quarter outperforming the benchmark. The largest contributors to weak returns in Technology were software names, where there continues to be fear that these stocks stand to be the most hurt by AI eating the world. Consumer Discretionary was the second-largest source of underperformance, with weakness spread across the portfolio's holdings. While the companies we own have diverse business models, they are all levered to consumer spending, where persistent spending fears, especially at the lower end of the income scale, have pressured valuations. Our underperformance in the Financials sector was concentrated in our two holdings that operate next-generation financial platforms, which offer customers the ability to purchase stocks, bonds, and cryptocurrencies. Within Health Care, three of our five period-end holdings underperformed the benchmark. The weakness was concentrated in our MedTech holdings, which includes our sole healthcare Software-as-a-Service position (see top detractors below), where the sector has fallen out of favor thus compressing valuations. Finally, in Producer Durables, our slight underperformance reflected multiple compression in our sole holding notwithstanding the company continuing to deliver strong fundamental momentum.

Contributors and Detractors

4Q25 TOP 5 Contributors (%) RETURN IMPACT
Intuitive Surgical (ISRG) 26.6 0.8
Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) 32.4 0.7
Alphabet (GOOGL) 28.9 0.5
Eli Lilly (LLY) 41.1 0.4
Amazon.com (AMZN) 5.1 0.4
4Q25 TOP 5 Detractors (%) RETURN IMPACT
ServiceNow (NOW) (16.8) (1.1)
Netflix (NFLX) (21.8) (0.9)
Veeva Systems (VEEV) (25.1) (0.7)
Meta (META) (10.0) (0.7)
Oracle (ORCL) (30.5) (0.7)

Top Contributors

Intuitive Surgical, Inc. (ISRG)

Intuitive Surgical is a pioneering medical technology company best known for its da Vinci robotic surgical system, which enables surgeons to perform precise, minimally invasive procedures. The system is widely used in urologic, gynecologic, general, and hernia repair surgeries, offering patients reduced pain, blood loss, and recovery time compared to traditional approaches. Intuitive generates revenue through three primary streams: system sales, instruments and accessories (such as scalpels, graspers, and cauterization tools), and services, which includes maintenance, training, and technical support, creating a high-margin recurring revenue model anchored by a growing global installed base of 10,200 units. The stock's fourth quarter outperformance was concentrated in a single day in which the company posted a massive earnings beat, besting consensus by ~5% on the top line and 20% on the bottom line. This beat sent the stock up 14%, with momentum carrying through to the end of the quarter.

Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD)

Advanced Micro Devices is a global leader in designing and manufacturing high-performance semiconductor products, including processors, graphics cards, and chipsets. The company develops CPUs and GPUs for a wide range of applications, such as gaming, data centers, AI, and high-performance computing. The company's competitive edge lies in its advanced CPU and GPU architectures, such as its "Zen" and "RDNA" designs, which offer exceptional performance, energy efficiency, and total cost of ownership. The stock price rose as the company posted strong earnings results in November that dovetailed with its mid-November analyst day during which management guided to a 35% revenue CAGR for the next several years driven by the company's soon-to-be launched MI450 and MI500 series products. These products will be the company's first rack scale systems, putting it in more direct competition with NVIDIA (NVDA), the industry's leader in rack scale architecture.

Alphabet Inc. (GOOG)

Alphabet is a global technology company that currently operates 9 products that have over 1B monthly active users. These products, which include Android, Chrome, Search, YouTube, Gmail, Play, Maps, Photos, and Drive, have all become global household names and serve to fuel the company's vast digital advertising ecosystem. In addition to this broad and deep ecosystem, the company has a rapidly growing cloud business, Google (GOOGL) Cloud Platform, which comprises a suite of computing services that Alphabet sells to businesses, governments, and developers. The company's competitive moat is built on powerful network effects and vertical integration, utilizing massive user data and proprietary AI hardware (TPUs) to deliver lower-cost, high-performance services that are difficult for competitors to replicate. The upward momentum in the fourth quarter was due to a shift in investor sentiment from viewing Google as an AI laggard to an AI powerhouse, combined with strong financial results and a favorable U.S. District Court ruling that was significantly better than feared.

Top Detractors

ServiceNow, Inc. (NOW)

Founded in 2004, ServiceNow has become the leading provider of cloud-based software solutions that define, structure, manage and automate workflow services for global enterprises. ServiceNow pioneered the use of the cloud to deliver IT service management (ITSM) applications. These applications allow users to manage incidents and to plan new IT projects, provision clouds, manage application performance and build applications themselves. The company has since expanded beyond the ITSM market to provide workflow solutions for IT operations management, customer support, human resources, security operations and other enterprise departments where previously a patchwork of semi-automated processes have typically been used with varying degrees of success. ServiceNow's stock sold off in the fourth quarter as investors priced in fears the software application layer at large could be disintermediated by AI native products. This fear drove meaningful multiple compression that materialized despite strong fundamental growth delivered by the company.

Netflix, Inc. (NFLX)

Netflix is the global leader in subscription-based streaming, delivering a vast library of films, television series, documentaries, and mobile games to over 300 million members across more than 190 countries. The company generates revenue primarily through tiered monthly subscription fees, offering both ad-supported and premium ad-free plans, while increasingly diversifying its income through advertising sales, content licensing, and live events. Its competitive moat is anchored by its massive global scale, proprietary data-driven algorithms that maximize user retention, and a multibillion-dollar annual investment in Netflix Originals that often capture the zeitgeist, leading to viral marketing and exclusive brand loyalty. Netflix's stock fell in the quarter due to two main headwinds. First, the

company made a surprise $83B bid to acquire Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD), which would require the company to take on $50B in new debt, sparking fears about excess leverage and a seeming departure from the company's traditional build-not-buy content acquisition strategy. Second, in the company's 3Q25 earnings announcement, management disclosed a surprise $620M tax charge stemming from a long-standing dispute with Brazilian authorities, leading to a miss on earnings per share expectations.

Veeva Systems Inc. (VEEV)

Since its inception, Veeva Systems has grown to become the leading SaaS provider of cloud solutions for the global life sciences industry. Veeva's industry-specific cloud solutions provide data, software, and services to address a broad range of needs, including multi-channel customer relationship management, content management, master data management, and customer data management. Veeva's products help its customers bring products to market faster while maintaining compliance with government regulations. Veeva's underperformance in the quarter followed its earnings announcement in mid-November. Even though the company beat its quarterly earnings and revenue estimates, management stated they are realistically targeting 14 of the top 20 global pharmaceutical companies for their new Vault CRM product, down from prior indications of 16 of the top 20. The market viewed this "loss" of two potential top-tier clients as a sign that competitors (like Salesforce (CRM)'s own new Life Sciences Cloud) were gaining material traction.

Risk Disclosure

Past performance is not indicative of future results. The performance shown is for the Ithaka US Growth Strategy Composite.

All fully discretionary taxable and non-taxable accounts are added to the composite following the first quarter in which their ending market values equal or exceed $0.5 million. Results of individual accounts may vary from the composite depending on account size, timing of transactions and market conditions prevailing at the time of the transaction. The gross-of-fee performance does not reflect the payment of management fees and other expenses that are incurred in the management of an account. The net-of-fee performance includes the payment of such fees and expenses. Gross-of-fee performance and net-of-fee performance both include the reinvestment of all distributions, dividends and other income.

The performance shown is compared to the Russell 1000 Growth Index and the S&P 500 TR Index.

The Russell 1000 Growth Index measures the performance of the broad growth segment of the U.S. equity universe. It includes those companies from the Russell 1000 Index with high price-to-book ratios and high forecasted growth as compared to other companies listed in the Russell 1000 Index. The S&P 500 TR Index is a market-capitalization-weighted index that measures the performance of 500 leading publicly traded companies in the U.S. The index tracks both the capital gains as well as any cash distributions, such as dividends or interest, attributed to the components of the index. These broad-based securities indexes are unmanaged and are not subject to fees and expenses typically associated with managed accounts. Individuals cannot invest directly in an index.The information provided in this report should not be considered a recommendation to purchase or sell any particular security. There is no assurance that any securities discussed herein will remain in an account’s portfolio at the time you receive this report or that securities sold have not been repurchased. The securities discussed do not represent an account’s entire portfolio and in the aggregate may represent only a small percentage of an account’s portfolio holdings. It should not be assumed that any of the securities transactions or holdings discussed were or will prove to be profitable, or that the investment recommendations or decisions Ithaka makes in the future will be profitable or will equal the investment performance of the securities discussed herein. Investing in securities entails risk and may result in loss of principal.

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Politics And The Markets 01/21/26
data.description:
This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

More on Today's Markets:

Moderation Guidelines:

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Personal attacks on another user account
  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation
  • Stereotyping, prejudiced or racist language about individuals or the topic under discussion.
  • Inciting violence messages, encouraging hate groups and political violence.

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

For any issue with regards to comments please email us at : moderation@seekingalpha.com.

Seeking Alpha's Disclosure: Past performance is no guarantee of future results. No recommendation or advice is being given as to whether any investment is suitable for a particular investor. Any views or opinions expressed above may not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker or US investment adviser or investment bank. Our analysts are third party authors that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body.

data.title: Regency Centers: Strong Dividend Growth, Shares Fairly Valued
data.description:
Regency Centers Corporation (REG) is a real estate investment trust ("REIT") that is currently trading at the upper end of its 52-week range but is down about 1% over the past year. While the returns are comparable to peers within the sector, the performance has significantly underperformed the broader markets.

I've provided coverage on REG in prior years, and my commentary has been consistent with regards to the REIT's portfolio composition and performance with regards to peers and the broader markets. In my last piece on REG, I noted that shares were fairly valued due to its strong balance sheet and attractive dividend payout.

Since my last update, shares have gained about 7%. While positive, the returns still pale in comparison to the broader S&P 500 (SPY), which is up about 65% in the same period.

Despite the underperformance of broader markets, I still view REG as a REIT worth paying attention to due to its strong portfolio metrics, positive dividend growth, and defensive nature. With markets trading near their highs, the defensive aspect of the portfolio could be especially attractive to those seeking to mitigate risk in their portfolios.

REG Stock Key Portfolio Metrics

Regency's portfolio is concentrated on open-air shopping centers located in suburban areas. These centers also have a grocery component, with 85% of the portfolio being anchored by a grocery store. I view this positively and see the grocery component as a defensive component of the portfolio that could safeguard against broader downturns elsewhere in the real estate market.

Among REG's top tenants are names such as Publix, Albertsons (ACI), and the TJX Companies (TJX), to name a few. Notably, no single tenant represents 3% or more of REG's total annualized base rent. This is a positive mark with regards to REG's tenant diversity, in my view.

Tenant quality is one reason why the overall portfolio has maintained a strong occupancy rate. As of the company's most recent investor presentation, the same property leased rate was 96%. Tenant retention and portfolio quality is likely one reason why REG has been able to command pricing power that is above the pack of peers and comparable to larger REITs, such as Federal Realty (FRT).

At 15x forward funds from operations ("FFO"), REG does trade at a premium to peers. FRT and Kite Realty Group (KRG), for example, trade at 14x and 11x, respectively.

The valuation is likely one reason why most Seeking Alpha ("SA") analysts view the stock as a "hold". While the SA quants do knock REG for the valuation as well, the quants are more bullish, due primarily to the REIT's growth and profitability prospects. Analysts on Wall Street, similarly, are bullish, with most seeing upside potential of over 10% in the stock.

REG Stock Operating Performance

In its most recent filing for Q3, REG demonstrated why its shares continue to command a premium valuation. Same-property NOI growth, for example, came in just under 5% for the quarter, with base rent growth doing most of the heavy lifting at roughly 4.7%. I view this growth as solid leasing execution, overall.

In addition, the portfolio also got a boost from accelerating rent commencements from the SNO pipeline and a tenant base that remains in very good shape.

On the occupancy front, overall occupancy ended the quarter at a healthy 96.4%, with commenced occupancy climbing to 94.4%, highlighting how much embedded growth is still flowing through the portfolio.

One standout was the consistency across the metrics. Cash re-leasing spreads of 13% and GAAP spreads north of 20% showed REG is still capturing meaningful mark-to-market rent growth, even late in the cycle.

Furthermore, bad debt remains historically low, tenant sales are holding up, and demand across anchors and shop space continues to exceed supply, a powerful setup in today's retail environment, in my view.

On the earnings side, management raised full-year guidance, now expecting same-property NOI growth of 5.25% to 5.5% and mid-7% growth in NAREIT FFO. That combination of strong NOI and steady FFO growth supports REG's reputation as a best-in-class operator, even if much of that quality is already reflected in the stock price, in my view.

REG Stock Dividend

REG currently provides a quarterly dividend of $0.7550/share. It's worth noting that this payout was recently increased from a previous quarterly payout of $0.7050/share as part of REG's Q3 earnings release. The increase marks the 5th straight increase since 2020, a testament to the earnings potential of the REIT.

From a safety perspective, I view the increase as sustainable, given the management team's forward guidance of a midpoint of $4.40/share for 2025 core operating earnings. The payment represents less than 70% of this midpoint, slightly below sector averages in the mid-70% range.

At current trading values, the dividend yields a respectable 4.2%. While this is not the highest-yielding dividend amongst the peer set, it still beats the returns offered through other cash and cash-like investments, in my view.

Is REG Stock a Buy, Sell, or Hold?

While I don't view REG as a screaming buy today, I still view the REIT as a strong watchlist contender for defensive-focused investors.

In my view, REG checks a lot of the boxes investors may find themselves searching for in uncertain environments. That is, a high-quality, grocery-anchored portfolio, strong tenant diversification, consistently high occupancy, and a management team that has proven it can drive organic NOI and FFO growth through multiple cycles. In my view, these traits give the portfolio a defensive character that shouldn't be overlooked, especially with broader equity markets trading near highs and macro risks clearly rising.

That said, the market is already aware of these strengths. At roughly 15x forward FFO, REG trades at a premium to several peers, such as FRT and KRG, and I view that valuation as fair rather than compelling. The recent 7% dividend increase supports the durability of the cash flows, but it doesn't necessarily show the stock as undervalued, in my opinion.

For income-focused investors or those looking to add defensiveness to a portfolio, I believe REG remains a very attractive watch. I'd be more bullish on shares during periods of market volatility or broader pullbacks, when the valuation better reflects its long-term risk-adjusted return profile. For now, I view REG as a "hold" and see it as fairly valued, well-run, and positioned to retain value if markets turn more defensive.

data.title: Kontoor Brands: Back In The Undervalued Zone
data.description:
After an impressive 57.3% stock price appreciation following the Q2 FY2025 earnings release, Kontoor Brands (KTB) has apparently lost its appeal to investors. Despite solid Q3 FY2025 numbers, an earnings beat, and an updated 2025 full-year outlook, selling pressure built, sending the share price back to where it was in August 2025. Those who missed the most recent summer discount have a chance to jump in and acquire an undervalued compounder with promising growth and a 3.43% dividend yield.

Reasons to Like Kontoor Brands

Some of the main characteristics that make the apparel manufacturer a great business, as described in the article Kontoor Brands: A Bargain In An Expensive Market, are its iconic brands, industry leadership, and strong cash flow generation. However, after the first reported quarter, including Helly Hansen's numbers and the stock price back in the undervalued zone, there is even more to like about the company and its potential as an investment.

Helly Hansen

The acquisition appears to be the catalyst Kontoor Brands needed to elevate its apparel business by adding a high-quality outdoor brand to its portfolio.

The first quarter since Helly Hansen's results were consolidated confirms it.

  • The brand contributed $185,930 in revenue, roughly equal to the Lee brand's in the same period.
  • From a full-year perspective, Helly Hansen is on track to generate $460.0 million, which is $5.0 million above prior estimates.
  • The business in China is expecting growth above 70.0% this year, which is particularly relevant amid the tariff shakeup.

The management's message is clear and consistent. The acquisition of Helly Hansen has been the right move, and as for now, it exceeds expectations.

Helly is headed into the fourth quarter with incredible momentum, and I could not be more confident in the opportunities ahead.

- Scott Baxter, CEO - Q3 FY 2025 results release

Yet, the newly acquired brand is not the only growth factor for the company. Excluding Helly Hansen, revenue in 2025 will increase by 2.0%, which aligns with a slow-and-steady total shareholder return (TSR) that Kontoor Brand has committed to since going public.

Wrangler & Lee

Wrangler grew sales by 2.0% YoY, translating into an impressive 19.0% growth in segment profit. It was possible thanks to the expansion of the brand's product line, including a wide range of denim styles. The strategy also helped Wrangler increase its market share and strengthen its competitive advantage. Chipping away at competitors is always a positive development. The focus on direct-to-consumer channels, ongoing international expansion (up 6.0% YoY), and strong organic growth in the U.S. are paying off, as can be seen in the numbers. Considering nine months of FY2025, Wrangler has grown its revenues by 4.0% and increased segment profit by 19.0%.

Wrangler's female business delivered another strong quarter, growing 20%, and the brand reinforced its position as the leading Western apparel name. Continued investment in brand visibility and demand creation supports momentum into 2026.

Although Lee posted revenue declines of 8.0% in Q3 and 14.0% for the nine months YoY in FY2025, there is light at the end of the tunnel, as the company carries out brand realignment. On a positive note, digital sales increased by 15.0% in the USA. The company reports that the results of the ongoing brand identity campaign are encouraging.

Both Wrangler and Lee have been part of the Big Three of high-quality denim for a very long time, and the overall outlook is uplifting. With Wrangler's market share gains and Lee's proactive efforts to gain momentum, the growth of both denim brands should match or exceed the denim market's growth rate.

Financials and Smart Management

From a financial standpoint, Kontoor Brands has a lot to be proud of. The management is laser-focused on leading the business in the right direction and keeping finances in check.

This approach has been evident in its free cash flow (FCF) management. An acquisition-induced debt accumulation seems to bother shareholders. Yet, the management addresses the problem and expresses a clear path the company will follow.

We are tracking ahead of our deleverage plan and expect to return to approximately 2x by year-end while consolidating a significant increase in earnings and cash flow.

Deleverage is our near-term priority, but we will take an offensive posture to deploy our cash generation to support our capital allocation framework, including our dividend and share repurchase programs.

The most recent acquisition has given the company an important boost to future earnings, margin expansion, and overall growth. Sales have reached their highest level since the spin-off and are expected to be at the upper end of the $3.00-$3.12 billion range. Although the adjusted gross margin fell to 45.78% in Q3, it remains at 46.07% TTM. While the adjusted operating margin excluding Helly Hansen grew 4.0%, the net margin on a GAAP basis declined to 7.68%.

Over the years, Kontoor Brands experienced tough times, such as the pandemic, which dragged the net margin to almost 0.0%. Yet thanks to smart decisions and risk management, profitability returned to a more sensible level, which it has remained at since. An event such as the acquisition always shakes a company's financials, and abrupt changes in margins should be expected. Despite that, Kontoor Brands keeps a healthy net margin of 7.68%, which will likely improve in the future.

Another positive sign is the company's Return On Invested Capital (ROIC), which indicates how well the business utilizes its capital to generate real profits.

In the case of Kontoor Brands, the ratio has remained at a satisfactory level for a long time before dropping following the acquisition, which should be a short-term event. Yet, even with ROIC at the lower end of the five-year period, investors should expect ample returns, as the legendary investor Charlie Munger once said.

If the business earns 6% on capital over 40 years and you hold it for that 40 years, you're not going to make much different than a 6% return—even if you originally buy it at a huge discount. Conversely, if a business earns 18% on capital over 20 or 30 years, even if you pay an expensive looking price, you'll end up with a fine result.

It emphasizes the importance of recognizing great businesses like Kontoor Brands. Besides that, his words point to another factor related to value investing, namely, a long-term horizon. The company's ROIC suggests faithful shareholders will be rewarded.

Valuation

The stock selloff seems unjustified and appears to reflect ongoing downward momentum driven by the overreaction to the Q3 financial results. Calculating the intrinsic value of Kontoor Brands and contrasting it with the current price is key to determining if this great business is also a great investment.

In the Discounted Earnings Model used for the calculation, the following estimates were used:

  • Kontoor Brands' EPS will be $4.28, $5.73, and $6.49 in 2025, 2026, and 2027, respectively, according to analysts.
  • Two scenarios were considered, both of which involved very conservative assumptions. In the worst-case scenario, the company's EPS growth drops by one-third in 2028 and again by one-third in 2029. Over the next five years, the business will increase profits at a 2.5% CAGR. In this scenario, the terminal P/E ratio is 9.0, which is a number for a company with no growth.
  • In the second scenario, Kontoor Brands' growth matches the denim jeans market growth for the next five years and grows at half the rate for the next five years. The terminal multiple is slightly higher and accounts for 12.0.
  • The discount rate is 10.0%, corresponding with the average long-term annual growth of the S&P 500 (SP500).

Assuming a disastrous scenario in which Kontoor Brands matches the estimated growth of the denim jeans market as a whole at 4.86%, the company enters the subsequent five-year period with growth of 2.43%, which is lower than the most recent inflation rate. Additionally, the very low terminal multiple further reduces the intrinsic value. However, with such pessimistic assumptions, the business's fair value is at $54.9. It would mean it's currently selling for what it's worth.

In the worst-case scenario, the analysts' numbers for the next three years are taken into account. The growth rate in the subsequent two years drops, and it enters the second five-year period with a 2.5% annual EPS increase, which is still lower than current inflation. Besides that, the terminal multiple suggests the company won't grow. These assumptions give an intrinsic value of $71.25, suggesting an upside of 23.2%. Kontoor Brands is undervalued.

Risks

Tariff risk with regard to China has deteriorated to the point that the subject no longer comes up when discussing Kontoor Brands. On the other hand, the danger of high customer concentration, mentioned in the previous article, remains, and it hasn't changed much in the last few months. All other risks mentioned by the company in the Risks section are real; they are likely to occur, though with a small probability in most cases.

What the market sees most likely right now is the risk of integrating Helly Hansen and achieving the expected growth. Kontoor Brands has been grounded in simplicity. This simplicity was embodied in the manufacturing and sales of the iconic brands Wrangler and Lee. Helly Hansen shakes things up with a new customer base, a different palette of products, more seasonality, and more complexity. Workwear and Outdoor is a new field for the company, and the latter is a highly competitive space. Yes, Helly Hansen has grown its revenues significantly over the years. However, legacy brands as well as some of the new names are going above and beyond to acquire new customers and gain market share.

Yet, the acquisition is part of a long-term strategy that the company has always been transparent about. Financial underperformance of the business as a whole might be real in the short term before the synergy costs are settled. The management is optimistic about the future of Kontoor Brands and its new asset, Helly Hansen.

Conclusion

Kontoor Brands is a great business and has proven it on many occasions since the spin-off. Now, the company enters a new phase, which is expected to combine solid organic performance and M&A. Helly Hansen is evidence of sticking to the strategy. This, together with the company's financial conservatism, has kept the business on track toward its long-term goals, while beating analysts' expectations and increasing total shareholder return.

The broad market is currently shaky, and its decline may drag the stock price of Kontoor Brands even lower. Regardless, the company is back in the undervalued zone, and the business is a buy for value investors who love great businesses at a discount.

data.title: What Happens When The January Effect Meets Midterm Elections?
data.description:
By Scott Bauer

Financial markets are influenced by numerous factors, including several notable seasonal patterns that may guide investor and trader behavior. These often-cited anomalies include the strategy to “Sell in May and Go Away,” routine “Quarter & Year-End Rebalancing,” the popular “Santa Claus Rally,” and of course, the historical “January Effect.”

The January Effect references historical data suggesting that market prices tend to rise more significantly in January than in any other month. This phenomenon was most pronounced among small-cap stocks from the 1940s through the mid-1970s. Its reliability began to wane from 1980 to 2000 and has been inconsistent since.

Traditional Drivers of the Effect

When the effect does manifest, it is typically attributed to behavioral and structural factors at the turn of the calendar year:

  1. Tax-loss harvesting: Investors may sell underperforming shares in December to create capital losses and then repurchase those same shares when the calendar flips over.
  2. Year-end bonus investments: Many people receive annual bonuses in December or January and choose to invest some of the proceeds in the stock market, potentially driving prices higher.
  3. Investor psychology: It’s a common notion that people tend to start new things at the beginning of the year, including putting money into the stock market. The “new year, new beginnings” mindset can be difficult to shrug off.

Midterm Elections: A Different Pattern Emerges

While the January Effect's influence has faded, the cycle of midterm election years introduces a distinct set of market dynamics. Over the last 20 years, January and February have not been all that bullish, averaging gains of 0.3% and 0.2%, respectively. The inherent uncertainty surrounding these elections has made midterm years challenging, with average returns falling below 5% since 1950.

There may be a silver lining as markets tend to follow a pattern around midterms.

  • Pre-Election Opportunity: Data from the last 90 years reveals that a significant drawdown in the S&P 500, typically occurring 12-18 months before midterm elections, has historically presented a compelling buying opportunity.
  • Post-Election Rally: Following the election, equities often demonstrate strong performance over the subsequent three, six and 12 months.

S&P 500 Performance Before and After U.S. Midterm Elections**

Volatility as a Key Indicator

Midterm election years are also notable for being the most volatile within the four-year presidential cycle. This heightened volatility, in my experience, stems from the market's aversion to uncertainty. Elections can significantly increase investor apprehension regarding potential policy shifts and their economic implications.

This increased uncertainty is clearly reflected in option markets. For instance, current observations show at-the-money implied volatility for January 2026 expiring options hovering around 13%. As we look further ahead to March, this implied volatility rises to over 15%, and it continues to climb toward 20% as the year progresses and midterm elections draw nearer.

The historical trend of increased uncertainty around midterm elections directly correlates with elevated equity volatility. This heightened implied volatility can be tracked by examining option chains in major indices such as the E-mini S&P 500 Options (ES), the E-mini Nasdaq-100 Options (NQ), and the E-mini Russell 2000 Options (RTO).

Original Post

data.title: First Year Of Trump's Second Term In The Books
data.description:
It has been one year since President Trump was sworn in for a second time. Looking back on the past year using our ETF asset class matrix below, the single best performing assets were precious metals, with gold (GLD) up 75.6% and silver (SLV) providing an astounding 210.6% total return.

While their gains didn't quite stack up to gold and silver, equities also had a respectable year. The major US indices all provided a total return ranging from high-single digits to the mid-teens with oversized gains from the small-cap Russell 2000 (RTY), large-cap growth (IVW), and the Nasdaq 100 (QQQ).

On a sector level, traditionally cyclical areas were mixed as the likes of Energy (XLE), Consumer Discretionary (XLY), and Financials (XLF) underperformed with mid single-digit gains, whereas Tech (XLK) and Industrials (XLI) both provided total returns surpassing 20%. Closing in on one month into the new year, there has been some rotation as XLE has rebounded while XLK is in the red.

While US equities did well in Trump's first year, it was internationals that were the stars in the equity space. Of the fourteen country ETFs shown in the matrix below, only two underperformed the S&P 500 (SPY). Those were Australia (EWA) with a marginal 2 percentage points of underperformance and more meaningful lagging performance from India (INDA).

Looking back to the first time Trump was sworn in back in 2017, the year following saw an even larger gain for the S&P 500 ETF (SPY) as it offered a 26.5% return in Trump's first year in the presidency. For this second term, SPY is again higher but by about ten percentage points less.

Taking it a step down, the only sector outperforming during this second term versus the first is Utilities (XLU), which is up 12.1% since last January; that compares to a 6.4% gain after the 2017 inauguration.

Finally, we would note that whereas in 2017 there was a significant cluster (seven of the eleven sectors) with similarly-sized gains between 25% and 30%, this time there is a more gradual increase across sectors. With that said, Tech was the top performing sector in both periods, and the margin was much larger during the first term.

Below we include a look at the S&P 500's individual best and worst stocks of the first year of Trump's first and second terms. As shown, this second term has seen larger losses and larger gains for the best and worst stocks. In fact, of the 15 best performers of the past year, 14 more than doubled compared to only five from 2017 to 2018.

Additionally, four stocks were more than cut in half this time around. Only Under Armor (UA) came close to that with a 49% loss after Trump's first inauguration.

Despite these big moves at the tail ends of the distribution, on the whole of S&P 500 members, average gains are smaller in the past year than they were from January 2017 to January 2018. Additionally, fewer stocks have risen (80% versus 65%).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Arthur J. Gallagher: Benefits Of $13.5 Bln Acquisition Of AssuredPartners
data.description:
By Anthony Goh, Senior Investment Research Analyst @ Khaveen Investments & Shivansh Prashant Mundra, Investment Research Intern @ Khaveen Investments

In this analysis, we cover Arthur J. Gallagher & Co. (AJG) to examine potential changes to the company after it acquired AssuredPartners for $13.45 billion last year. We believe the deal is positive for the firm, as we anticipate the combined company to benefit from increased scale and market share, and expect its market share to increase by 3% in the highly competitive Insurance Brokers subindustry. While the deal is expected to realize revenue synergies, it is minimal at only 0.42% of revenue, but the greater benefit to AJG is the strengthening mix in the P&C (Property & Casualty), retail, and specialty insurance business that AJG has major exposure to.

Scale and Market Share Impact

In this section, we analyze the impact of the acquisition on the market share of AJG. We will examine its market share in the Insurance Brokerage subindustry and analyze whether the company can overtake its competitors.

Analyzing the change in the market share of AJG after acquiring AssuredPartners, we calculated that its market share in the Insurance Brokerage subindustry is expected to increase from 10% to 13% based on the revenues of both companies. We calculated the market share for AssuredPartners to be around 2.64% by taking the ratio of the company’s revenue, which was $2.9 billion, to the total revenue in the subindustry, estimated at $110 billion. Even though MMC (MRSH) and Aon (AON) remain the clear market leaders, the acquisition strengthens AJG’s position by narrowing the gap with Aon and widening its lead over mid-tier players such as WTW (WTW), Alliant Insurance, and Brown & Brown (BRO). However, AJG’s remains in the number three position behind MMC and Aon which are larger players.

We thus believe the outlook for AJG following the acquisition is positive as the deal strengthens its positioning in the Insurance Brokerage subindustry, and narrows the gap with Aon while pulling ahead of mid-tier players. That said, the Insurance Brokerage subindustry remains highly competitive among the top players. The main benefit of the acquisition is it strengthens AJG’s scale, as well as enhances its exposure specifically in the retail, P&C, and specialty insurance brokerage businesses, which we examine in the next point.

Revenue Diversification

We analyze how the breakdown of revenue by business segments changes post-acquisition. We also look at the expected synergies guided by management to be realized by the company as they operate in similar market segments.

Before the acquisition, AJG’s revenue mix was already fairly diverse, whereas AssuredPartners’ revenues were primarily in Brokerage with a focus on Retail P/C and Benefits. After the acquisition, the combined revenue share of the two companies shows both the Retail P/C and Retail Benefits segments slightly higher, whereas Risk Management and Reinsurance fall moderately. This increases AJG’s concentration in its core brokerage operations while reducing the relative weight of non-brokerage segments.

We find that both AJG and AssuredPartners have relatively similar revenues, with both companies concentrating on Retail P/C and Employee Benefits. We believe that the acquisition could lead to synergies for revenues, as both companies have overlapping core segments, which can help APG to increase its customer base and cross-selling abilities. AssuredPartners’ also has a large distribution network, which can further support cross-selling opportunities. AJG also unlocks cross-selling opportunities and deepens its US middle market penetration. According to the company, the AssuredPartners acquisition is likely to create revenue synergies of about $60 million, though this is fairly minimal at 0.42% of AJG’s revenue.

Profitability Margin and Cost Synergies

In the last section, we finally analyze the impact AssuredPartners has had on the operating margin of AJG. We also examine the impact that cost synergies and integration costs have on the margin.

Based on the company’s presentation, AssuredPartners has an operating margin of 30%, which is higher compared to AJG’s standalone company operating margin of 20.9%. While the acquisition benefits the company’s margins, we calculated the combined margin to be 22.9%, which is only 2% points higher compared to AJG’s margin before the acquisition. Also worth noting, the company highlighted acquisition integration costs of $140 million in 2025 ($500 million guided over 3 years), which consist of $70 million relating to workforce and $70 million relating to operating expenses, which would weigh on margins. However, AJG also guided cost synergies of $100 million, primarily due to back-office consolidation, IT system efficiencies, real estate optimization, and expanded use of the Gallagher Center of Excellence. After factoring in all these, we calculate the company’s margin to be 23.1% in 2025, which is 2.2 percentage points higher compared to AJG’s standalone TTM margin.

We expect the outlook for AJG’s profitability following the acquisition is constructive, as its higher operating margins should lift AJG’s margins modestly. That said, both companies are already large players in the insurance brokerage industry and enjoy relatively high margins, consistent with what we highlighted in our previous analysis of MMC, where scale is a key driver of profitability. For insurance brokers, the largest expense is COGS in the form of salaries and employee benefits, given the service-based business model, which limits the scope for margin expansion. While the company highlighted $100 million in cost synergies from the deal, we view this as relatively minimal compared to the size of the combined company and note that integration costs of $140 million will partially offset the benefits. Overall, while we expect AJG’s margin to rise from 20.9% to 23.1% in 2025, we believe the incremental uplift is limited, and profitability gains beyond this level may be harder to realize.

Risk: Higher Debt Levels Post Acquisition

AJG’s debt has grown from $2.5 billion to $8.3 billion, as the company has raised $8.5 billion to fund its AssuredPartners’ acquisition. This led to a sharp rise in debt ($13.7 billion), causing its cash-to-debt ratio to fall from 1.1x in 2024 to 0.1x based on its Q3 financials. This may pose a risk to its financial capability to pursue large deals in the near future, and will likely focus on smaller deals and organic growth for additional growth beyond this acquisition.

Valuation

We forecasted AJG’s growth projections using its historical average, whereas for AssuredPartners, we apply its 3-year organic growth of 6%. We also included revenue synergies modestly for our forecasts ($60 million annually). We assumed revenue growth of 19.8% in 2025. Given that the deal was completed in August 2025, we also prorated the contribution of AssuredPartners for four months. Over the 5-year forecast horizon, we calculated AJG’s forward average revenue growth as 16.1%.

For our valuation, we used a DCF as the company has stable positive FCF margins. We accounted for the combined company revenue and profitability by growing its margins by 2.2%. We also referenced the 5-year average EV/EBITDA multiple of leading insurance brokers, excluding outliers, which is 17.63x. Based on this, our model shows an overall upside of 15.85%.

Verdict

The AssuredPartners acquisition is the largest deal in the insurance brokerage subindustry and increases AJG’s market share to 13%, making it an even larger player in the competitive Insurance Brokers subindustry. The segments are also quite synergistic, as both companies are concentrated on Retail P/C and Employee Benefits. However, the acquisition will likely only have limited revenue synergies of $60 million, less than 0.5% of revenue. The acquisition can also help improve AJG’s operating margin from 20.9% to 23.1% in 2025, due to its higher margins. We had also taken into account of $140 million in integration costs along with $100 million in cost synergies to calculate AJG’s combined margins. Based on our DCF valuation, we rate the company as a Buy with a modest upside of 15.85% from current price, as we see the acquisition to overall be beneficial to AJG's scale and market positioning, supporting higher revenues and profitability, and we will continue to monitor management commentary and guidance on the business combination and synergy progress in its upcoming earnings call on 29 January.

data.title: Rates Spark: Yields Have Spiked, But Levels Are Far From Overly Elevated
data.description:
By Padhraic Garvey, CFA, Regional Head of Research, Americas; Michiel Tukker, Senior European Rates Strategist; and Benjamin Schroeder, Senior Rates Strategist

Rationalising the rise in Treasury yields is more simple than geopolitics

The rise in Treasury yields since Friday reflects a combination of factors. Talk on Friday that President Trump might hold on to Kevin Hassett is not one of them. It was a spark for sure, but not the driver. Remember, Kevin Hassett has not been the front-runner for a number of weeks now, as he was overtaken by the other Kevin (Warsh).

Also, the market had never discounted a super-dovish Fed in the first place. And for good reason, as the mathematics never added up that way. So, bond yields spiking on the Hassett story never made complete sense, apart from it being a spark that lit up a path of least resistance for higher yields (for many other reasons).

Then we turn to Tuesday's movements. Now the thing is, global markets were open on Monday, and there was no particular new news on the Greenland 'back and forth' between the US and Europe by Tuesday. All was known on Monday.

Even Japanese yields rose dramatically on Monday, but global yields did not react, at least not like they reacted on Tuesday. The only difference between Monday and Tuesday is the US was closed on Monday. And yes, in the background, Japanese yields had spiked again, except this time, prompting some associated US dollar weakness, and then, higher US yields.

It's not that the Hassett story and the Greenland threats are not relevant. They are; it's just that nothing has actually happened in this space, apart from words being spoken, followed by lots of supposition. But there is other background noise that is proving far more relevant.

First is the reality of elevated inflation and elevated deficits as an issue that has not garnered the fear that it should have, especially in the US. The moves in Japanese Government Bonds (JGBs) act to remind Treasuries that they too have issues on both of these fronts.

The 30yr JGB yield is now just short of 4%, and the 30yr US Treasury yield is just short of 5%. And why not? After all, Japanese inflation is at 3%, and US inflation technically rounds up to 3%.

On the fiscal side, it's the rising interest rate costs that concern Japan the most, on an already eye-watering high debt/GDP ratio. While in the US, it is more about the absolute size of the fiscal deficit. The delta has swung on the deficit, which is good, and has allowed swap spreads to narrow. But underlying fiscal problems remain.

Japan is the trigger now for the eurozone, but fiscal concerns are a global thing

The back end of the euro curve is being driven by global spillovers, but the front end remains remarkably stable. Bund yields shot up alongside the sell-off in JGBs, but over the course of the day, the impact was a mere 2bp for the 10Y.

Markets now price in a 25% probability of another European Central Bank rate cut this year, only mildly higher than earlier this month. These market moves suggest the eurozone’s macro picture has not really changed much and that most of the moves can be attributed to the JGB volatility.

As government spending concerns keep playing across the world, the path of least resistance is still for steeper curves. Japan is not the only country facing fiscal challenges, and as global rates rise, budget deficits will only be strained further.

This environment makes it difficult to find safe havens in government bonds, and therefore gold being the big winner this week should not be a surprise. Our FX strategists also pointed out that currencies, especially from countries with healthy government finances – specifically NOK, SEK and CHF – did well during this bond sell-off.

Wednesday’s events and market view

Amid geopolitical tensions, all eyes will be on Davos, where Trump is expected to speak at 14:30 CET. Data will be secondary, also given the very few releases of note after the UK CPI in the European morning.

The US will release MBA mortgage application numbers and pending home sales. There won’t be any relevant releases out of the eurozone, but the ECB’s Lagarde, Villeroy and Nagel will speak at Davos.

Primary markets remain busy with syndicated deals from Austria (new 10y benchmark and green bond tap), Finland (new 15y) and Cyprus (new 10y) alongside scheduled auction supply from Germany in 15y and 30y bonds (€2bn). The UK will auction 3y gilts.

The main focus will likely fall onto the 20y UST auction (US$13bn) as markets revert to looking for any evidence of a 'Sell America' narrative.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: Accenture: Risk-Reward Setup Has Turned Positive (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Investment summary

My previous investment thought on Accenture PLC (ACN) was a downgrade to a hold rating because I wanted to wait for bookings and growth visibility to improve before turning bullish again. That evidence has now arrived. Bookings have recovered nicely, the mix has improved, and GenAI bookings are becoming a more tangible growth driver. With valuation still undemanding relative to the market, I believe the risk-reward has turned positive.

Turnaround in Bookings

My biggest worry previously was that bookings were rolling over, and that would eventually flow through to revenue. The underlying concern was that clients were pulling back, new work was slowing, and the pipeline was shrinking at a pace that made the long-term AI story irrelevant in the near term. Fast forward to today; I think the past two quarters' numbers have proved me wrong. Take the latest Q1 2026 data, for instance. ACN reported a massive bookings rebound, with new bookings up 12% y/y to $20.9 billion, marking a sharp turnaround vs. the 7% decline previously. For the same reason I was worried about revenue growth when bookings were weak, I am now confident that revenue growth will be better than I expected since bookings are up.

What gives more confidence is the quality of that recovery. While the headline number of 33 client bookings over $100 million is only a modest increase from the previous 30, the underlying signal has improved dramatically. In my last update, those large deals happened when total bookings were down 7% y/y, implying clients are likely shifting budgets from less important projects to larger ones. Now, the story has changed. These 33 large deals happened with total bookings up 12%, indicating that the "leakage" from the rest of the business has stopped, or in other words, clients have increased spending appetite across all projects' size and importance.

Pivot to Agentic AI, Capital Deployment

The recovery in bookings is the first layer that de-risks the earnings profile. The layer that drives further upside is the AI strategy. I argued that GenAI bookings were growing but still too small to matter at the P&L level back in July '25. Clearly, I have underestimated the magnitude of demand because new GenAI bookings have reached $2.2 billion in Q1 2026, now more than 10% of total bookings. At this pace of growth, GenAI bookings could easily be >20% by the end of 2026. The quality of these bookings has also gotten better, leading me to think the contribution at the revenue level is going to be much more apparent vs. bookings. The recent GenAI bookings are no longer just for basic use cases like content creation and chat interfaces but are for agentic AI.

This changes the dynamic significantly because these are autonomous systems that can run complex workflows, make decisions, and deliver tangible and measurable cost savings. This should give ACN a lot more pricing power as a result. And it gets better if you consider that switching costs are materially higher because once these agents are embedded into the core workflows of clients (like inventory management, demand forecasting, or customer support), it is extremely disruptive to operations if they want to replace the vendor (think about the efforts to retrain, pull out everything, and reimplement, all the while when the business is still operating).

There is also a strategic acquisition angle that makes ACN GenAI-related growth more durable. Faculty is a good example. This acquisition brings in more than 400 specialized AI professionals and decision intelligence capabilities directly in-house. With a larger pool of AI professionals, ACN can move beyond simple implementation to addressing more complex/customized implementation for its clients (i.e., move up the value chain).

As such, I am positive that management is doubling down on this strategy, with intentions to deploy ~$3 billion on acquisitions in FY2026 (~2x the pace of FY2025). This strategy does come with a cost, but I view this as a rational trade-off. Management is basically choosing to give up near-term margin to secure its long-term growth runway. The question is whether ACN has the balance sheet strength to fund this, and I think the answer is yes. ACN ended the Q1 2026 quarter with a massive cash balance of $9.6 billion (net cash position of ~$4.5 billion). That, plus the fact that ACN generated free cash flow of $1.5 billion in the quarter alone, means they surely have ample firepower to fund this strategy.

Valuation

The last reason for my upgrade to a buy rating is because the stock has become relatively cheap. When I downgraded the stock previously, the valuation looked cheap, but I believed it deserved to be cheap. Today, the setup is different. ACN still trades near its 10-year low, at a ~10% discount to the S&P 500, but the operating data has clearly shown a solid recovery.

It feels to me that the market is not pricing this reality accurately and is focusing on the muted growth at the industry level. Per Morgan Stanley's recent CIO surveys, overall IT services budget growth is expected to be just 2% in 2026, so I can get why the market is feeling uneasy. But ACN numbers show that its growth is not coupled with industry growth since bookings are up 12%.

This is why I see a clear mispricing. At the current multiple of ~20x forward PE, the margin of safety leans in favor of investors since it is already priced below the market, and the consensus is not really expecting growth acceleration (still mid-to-high single digits). But we know that bookings are at 12% y/y, which would eventually flow through to topline growth. If bookings stay at this level, it is only a matter of time before the market re-rates the multiple higher to reflect this growth strength. The first layer of upside is when ACN re-rates back to 1x vs. the S&P 500 index (that is, 10% upside); the next is when the market re-rates ACN back to its historical premium vs. the index, at ~1.3x (that is, another 30% on top of the 10%).

Risk

While the bookings recovery is good, there is execution risk to consider. Management is aggressively deploying capital (~$3 billion) into M&A while simultaneously restructuring the legacy workforce. This could lead to integration challenges, particularly in retaining AI talents (which are in hot demand today) from acquisitions like Faculty. Furthermore, if the revenue ramp from these acquisitions comes in slower than expected, the margin could be compressed longer than expected, which could weigh on the multiple.

Conclusion

My view for ACN is an upgrade to a buy rating. The recovery in bookings removes a key overhang on revenue visibility, while the pivot toward agentic AI creates a tangible path to faster growth. Management's aggressive capital deployment is also something I am positive about. With the stock still trading below the market despite the improving fundamentals, I see valuation providing downside protection and multiple expansion offering upside as growth re-accelerates.

data.title: The Ithaka Group Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

U.S. equity markets concluded the fourth quarter in positive territory, though the modest gains belie a period of significant intra-quarter volatility. While the S&P 500 (SPX) and Nasdaq 100 (NDX) advanced 2.4% and 2.6% respectively, the Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJI) led the major indices with a more robust 3.6% return. Conversely, the Russell 1000 Growth (RLG) Index (R1000G) lagged slightly, rising just 1.1% as investors rotated away from high-growth technology names in favor of more defensive and value-oriented sectors. Despite the year ending on a high note, the markets had to continually climb the proverbial "wall of worry." Central to these concerns was the record-breaking 43-day government shutdown, which dampened risk appetite and clouded market transparency by delaying the release of critical economic data. Although the federal government reopened in mid-November, the temporary nature of the continuing resolution, which only funds operations through January 30th, leaves a lingering policy overhang. Compounding these fiscal concerns was a renewed debate regarding AI valuations. Investors increasingly questioned the sustainability of massive capital expenditures in artificial intelligence and the potential impact a spending slowdown might have on the "early winners" of this new industrial revolution. Despite these valuation headwinds, the AI megatrend remains a vital secular tailwind, with the sheer scale of global investment in this space continuing to provide a significant buffer for the broader economy (see chart below of Real Private Nonresidential Fixed Investment).

The quarter ended on a constructive note, however, as corporate fundamentals remained resilient. With approximately 83% of S&P 500 companies reporting positive earnings surprises, there are encouraging signs that the economic recovery is finally broadening beyond the narrow leadership of the "Magnificent Seven."

On the monetary policy front, the Federal Reserve maintained its easing cycle by implementing two 25-basis point (BP) cuts during the fourth quarter. These moves brought the federal funds rate to a range of 3.50% to 3.75% by year-end. Notably, the Fed also elected to conclude its quantitative tightening (QT) program in response to emerging liquidity strains within the short-term repo market. This shift signals that bank reserves have transitioned from "abundant" to merely "ample." Since this policy pivot one month ago, the Fed's balance sheet has expanded by approximately $100 billion, reaching a total of $6.6 trillion. Looking forward, traditional forecasts regarding rate trajectories and economic health have been largely overshadowed by speculation surrounding who will be appointed the next Federal Reserve Chairman. With Chairman Powell's term set to expire in May 2026, a nomination is widely anticipated within the first quarter. While several candidates have been discussed, President Trump's stated preference is for a successor who favors aggressive rate reductions to provide "rocket fuel" for domestic economic growth. Amidst this evolving macro and political backdrop, our investment discipline remains steadfast. We continue to focus on long-term wealth creation by maintaining concentrated positions in businesses with a high probability of compounding economic value. Our core criteria remain unchanged: we seek companies led by prudent management teams, supported by durable secular tailwinds, and built upon advantaged business models with superior unit economics in large expanding markets.

4Q25 Performance

Performance (%) 4Q25 1 YR 3 YR 5 YR ITD 1
Ithaka US Growth Strategy (Gross) (5.9) 5.1 29.1 8.9 17.2
Ithaka US Growth Strategy (NET) (6.1) 4.5 28.3 8.3 16.7
Russell 1000 Growth ("R1000G") 1.1 18.6 31.2 15.3 17.7
S&P 500 TR Index 2.7 17.9 23.0 14.4 14.8
1 ITD = inception-to-date, annualized. Inception date is 1/1/2009.

In a slightly positive market, Ithaka's portfolio lagged the R1000G during the fourth quarter, trailing the index by 700bps (-5.9% to +1.1%, gross of fees). Stock selection detracted 740 bps to relative performance, which was slightly offset by a 40bp positive impact from sector allocation. The portfolio demonstrated weak breadth and depth with only 8 of the 29 stocks held for the entire quarter, representing 28% of the names and 26% of the total weighting, outperforming the benchmark.

At the sector level, Ithaka generated positive relative returns in only one of the six major growth sectors where we maintain active exposure: Materials and Processing. Outperformance in materials was due to a sole holding purchased in the quarter outperforming the index. Our largest area of underperformance was Technology where the underperformance was pervasive, with only 4 of the 15 positions held for the entire quarter outperforming the benchmark. The largest contributors to weak returns in Technology were software names, where there continues to be fear that these stocks stand to be the most hurt by AI eating the world. Consumer Discretionary was the second-largest source of underperformance, with weakness spread across the portfolio's holdings. While the companies we own have diverse business models, they are all levered to consumer spending, where persistent spending fears, especially at the lower end of the income scale, have pressured valuations. Our underperformance in the Financials sector was concentrated in our two holdings that operate next-generation financial platforms, which offer customers the ability to purchase stocks, bonds, and cryptocurrencies. Within Health Care, three of our five period-end holdings underperformed the benchmark. The weakness was concentrated in our MedTech holdings, which includes our sole healthcare Software-as-a-Service position (see top detractors below), where the sector has fallen out of favor thus compressing valuations. Finally, in Producer Durables, our slight underperformance reflected multiple compression in our sole holding notwithstanding the company continuing to deliver strong fundamental momentum.

Contributors and Detractors

4Q25 TOP 5 Contributors (%) RETURN IMPACT
Intuitive Surgical (ISRG) 26.6 0.8
Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) 32.4 0.7
Alphabet (GOOGL) 28.9 0.5
Eli Lilly (LLY) 41.1 0.4
Amazon.com (AMZN) 5.1 0.4
4Q25 TOP 5 Detractors (%) RETURN IMPACT
ServiceNow (NOW) (16.8) (1.1)
Netflix (NFLX) (21.8) (0.9)
Veeva Systems (VEEV) (25.1) (0.7)
Meta (META) (10.0) (0.7)
Oracle (ORCL) (30.5) (0.7)

Top Contributors

Intuitive Surgical, Inc. (ISRG)

Intuitive Surgical is a pioneering medical technology company best known for its da Vinci robotic surgical system, which enables surgeons to perform precise, minimally invasive procedures. The system is widely used in urologic, gynecologic, general, and hernia repair surgeries, offering patients reduced pain, blood loss, and recovery time compared to traditional approaches. Intuitive generates revenue through three primary streams: system sales, instruments and accessories (such as scalpels, graspers, and cauterization tools), and services, which includes maintenance, training, and technical support, creating a high-margin recurring revenue model anchored by a growing global installed base of 10,200 units. The stock's fourth quarter outperformance was concentrated in a single day in which the company posted a massive earnings beat, besting consensus by ~5% on the top line and 20% on the bottom line. This beat sent the stock up 14%, with momentum carrying through to the end of the quarter.

Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD)

Advanced Micro Devices is a global leader in designing and manufacturing high-performance semiconductor products, including processors, graphics cards, and chipsets. The company develops CPUs and GPUs for a wide range of applications, such as gaming, data centers, AI, and high-performance computing. The company's competitive edge lies in its advanced CPU and GPU architectures, such as its "Zen" and "RDNA" designs, which offer exceptional performance, energy efficiency, and total cost of ownership. The stock price rose as the company posted strong earnings results in November that dovetailed with its mid-November analyst day during which management guided to a 35% revenue CAGR for the next several years driven by the company's soon-to-be launched MI450 and MI500 series products. These products will be the company's first rack scale systems, putting it in more direct competition with NVIDIA (NVDA), the industry's leader in rack scale architecture.

Alphabet Inc. (GOOG)

Alphabet is a global technology company that currently operates 9 products that have over 1B monthly active users. These products, which include Android, Chrome, Search, YouTube, Gmail, Play, Maps, Photos, and Drive, have all become global household names and serve to fuel the company's vast digital advertising ecosystem. In addition to this broad and deep ecosystem, the company has a rapidly growing cloud business, Google (GOOGL) Cloud Platform, which comprises a suite of computing services that Alphabet sells to businesses, governments, and developers. The company's competitive moat is built on powerful network effects and vertical integration, utilizing massive user data and proprietary AI hardware (TPUs) to deliver lower-cost, high-performance services that are difficult for competitors to replicate. The upward momentum in the fourth quarter was due to a shift in investor sentiment from viewing Google as an AI laggard to an AI powerhouse, combined with strong financial results and a favorable U.S. District Court ruling that was significantly better than feared.

Top Detractors

ServiceNow, Inc. (NOW)

Founded in 2004, ServiceNow has become the leading provider of cloud-based software solutions that define, structure, manage and automate workflow services for global enterprises. ServiceNow pioneered the use of the cloud to deliver IT service management (ITSM) applications. These applications allow users to manage incidents and to plan new IT projects, provision clouds, manage application performance and build applications themselves. The company has since expanded beyond the ITSM market to provide workflow solutions for IT operations management, customer support, human resources, security operations and other enterprise departments where previously a patchwork of semi-automated processes have typically been used with varying degrees of success. ServiceNow's stock sold off in the fourth quarter as investors priced in fears the software application layer at large could be disintermediated by AI native products. This fear drove meaningful multiple compression that materialized despite strong fundamental growth delivered by the company.

Netflix, Inc. (NFLX)

Netflix is the global leader in subscription-based streaming, delivering a vast library of films, television series, documentaries, and mobile games to over 300 million members across more than 190 countries. The company generates revenue primarily through tiered monthly subscription fees, offering both ad-supported and premium ad-free plans, while increasingly diversifying its income through advertising sales, content licensing, and live events. Its competitive moat is anchored by its massive global scale, proprietary data-driven algorithms that maximize user retention, and a multibillion-dollar annual investment in Netflix Originals that often capture the zeitgeist, leading to viral marketing and exclusive brand loyalty. Netflix's stock fell in the quarter due to two main headwinds. First, the

company made a surprise $83B bid to acquire Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD), which would require the company to take on $50B in new debt, sparking fears about excess leverage and a seeming departure from the company's traditional build-not-buy content acquisition strategy. Second, in the company's 3Q25 earnings announcement, management disclosed a surprise $620M tax charge stemming from a long-standing dispute with Brazilian authorities, leading to a miss on earnings per share expectations.

Veeva Systems Inc. (VEEV)

Since its inception, Veeva Systems has grown to become the leading SaaS provider of cloud solutions for the global life sciences industry. Veeva's industry-specific cloud solutions provide data, software, and services to address a broad range of needs, including multi-channel customer relationship management, content management, master data management, and customer data management. Veeva's products help its customers bring products to market faster while maintaining compliance with government regulations. Veeva's underperformance in the quarter followed its earnings announcement in mid-November. Even though the company beat its quarterly earnings and revenue estimates, management stated they are realistically targeting 14 of the top 20 global pharmaceutical companies for their new Vault CRM product, down from prior indications of 16 of the top 20. The market viewed this "loss" of two potential top-tier clients as a sign that competitors (like Salesforce (CRM)'s own new Life Sciences Cloud) were gaining material traction.

Transactions

During the quarter we initiated three new positions, Howmet (HWM), Broadcom (AVGO), and Credo (CRDO) and eliminated four positions, Blackstone (BX), e.l.f. Beauty (ELF), Marvell (MRVL), and The Trade Desk (TTD). Our trailing 12-month turnover increased to 22.1% while our trailing 3-year average annual turnover increased to 16.0%. 1

Market Outlook

Ithaka claims no expertise in economic or market predictions, and top-down analysis merely plays a supporting role in our approach to investing. We instead take our cues on the economy and the markets from our portfolio companies' management teams, as they discuss their business prospects, and industry outlooks, during quarterly calls. During the fourth quarter 93% of our full-period portfolio holdings beat top-line expectations, while 86% beat bottom-line expectations. Strong fundamental performance was not rewarded with commensurate strong stock price gains, with the average stock falling 2.4%, two names rising greater than 5% and twelve falling greater than 5%. Stock movements around earnings reports were negatively skewed but, per usual, included fat tails. In our last letter, we explored the staggering scale of the AI infrastructure buildout, an investment that rivals the greatest engineering projects in human history. As we close out 2025, the narrative is subtly but distinctly shifting: we believe 2026 will be the year of the “Show Me” phase of the AI cycle. While we are not at the point where AI-driven revenue has even partially, let alone fully, offset the massive capital expenditures of the hyperscalers, the gap between infrastructure spend and utility seems to be narrowing. Our management teams are no longer just discussing potential applications or testing of new services; they are actually reporting the first tangible fruits of their labor, suggesting that this buildout is not a monument to excess, but the necessary foundation for a new era of productivity.

Three years after the ChatGPT moment, many original predictions regarding explosive revenues flowing into the early enablers at the application layer have fallen short. OpenAI appears to be leading, and is reportedly on track for $20B in annual recurring revenue (ARR), Anthropic is projected to generate ~ $9B, Perplexity is on track for $150M, with a handful of other companies achieving more modest revenues numbering in the mere hundreds of millions. Even when annualized and combined, these figures pale in comparison to the $1.1T of CapEx that has already been spent over the past three years. So where are we?

Despite the scarcity of direct hard dollar revenues at the application layer, there are numerous examples of AI’s impact on the internal ROI’s for companies using these large language models (LLMs) to increase the productivity of their businesses. These stealth ROI’s are appearing across companies and sectors. Dara Khosrowshahi, CEO of Uber (UBER), has pointed to agentic AI workflows that optimized driver routing and consumer pricing in real time, with the latest generation of AI models translating into hundreds of millions of dollars of benefit for the company. John Plant, CEO of Howmet Aerospace (HWM), has highlighted AI as a dual-force for his company, both a technological tool to improve manufacturing efficiency and as a massive market driver for their industrial products. Plant has repeatedly praised the company’s digital thread, an AI-monitored manufacturing process that is significantly improving casting yields for advanced jet engine parts. Eli Lilly (LLY)’s CEO, David Ricks, frequently emphasizes that the number of possible organic chemicals that could become medicine is larger than the number of stars in the sky. Machine learning will allow the company to identify pathogenic cells and design targeted molecules, allowing Lilly to move from getting lucky to predictive engineering. In late 2025 Shopify (SHOP) reported traffic increased 7x for merchants who fully integrated AI powered tools into their websites and that these merchants saw a minimum 22% increase in conversion rates. Meta Platforms (META) CEO, Mark Zuckerberg, stated that AI-powered recommendation models drove a 5% increase in conversions on Instagram and a 3% increase on Facebook in the quarter, translating to hundreds of millions, if not billions, of advertising dollar impact. All of these examples, and many more that could be enumerated, demonstrate the true effect the AI revolution is having on global business operations. As we move into 2026, it is reasonable to expect the compounding nature of these productivity gains will lead to new and exciting products and services. In other words, the main economic opportunities being uncovered by the advent of AI should continue to broaden beyond the insatiable demand for accelerated computing and the requisite ongoing buildout in AI infrastructure, and migrate rapidly up the application stack.

As always, we end this letter acknowledging that one’s ability to digest, forecast, and accurately get one’s arms around the world is incredibly difficult, and we therefore choose to stay fully invested and focused on our mission of creating wealth for our clients by owning, in size, the great growth stories of our day.

Past performance is not indicative of future results. The performance shown is for the Ithaka US Growth Strategy Composite. All fully discretionary taxable and non-taxable accounts are added to the composite following the first quarter in which their ending market values equal or exceed $0.5 million. Results of individual accounts may vary from the composite depending on account size, timing of transactions and market conditions prevailing at the time of the transaction. The gross-of-fee performance does not reflect the payment of management fees and other expenses that are incurred in the management of an account. The net-of-fee performance includes the payment of such fees and expenses. Gross-of-fee performance and net-of-fee performance both include the reinvestment of all distributions, dividends and other income.

The performance shown is compared to the Russell 1000 Growth Index and the S&P 500 TR Index. The Russell 1000 Growth Index measures the performance of the broad growth segment of the U.S. equity universe. It includes those companies from the Russell 1000 Index with high price-to-book ratios and high forecasted growth as compared to other companies listed in the Russell 1000 Index. The S&P 500 TR Index is a market-capitalization-weighted index that measures the performance of 500 leading publicly traded companies in the U.S. The index tracks both the capital gains as well as any cash distributions, such as dividends or interest, attributed to the components of the index. These broad-based securities indexes are unmanaged and are not subject to fees and expenses typically associated with managed accounts. Individuals cannot invest directly in an index.
